content.text: Signed bearer tokens are the standard mechanism for authenticating HTTP requests in distributed systems. JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) encode identity and permission claims directly inside the token — no server-side session lookup required — making them a natural fit for stateless microservices, agent APIs, and service-to-service calls. This document covers how JWTs are structured, signed, validated, and safely used in agentic AI deployments.
is an open standard (RFC 7519) for representing claims between two parties as a compact, URL-safe string. It consists of three Base64URL-encoded segments separated by dots:
. The header identifies the signing algorithm; the payload carries structured claims about the subject; the signature proves the token was issued by a trusted party and has not been tampered with.

is any token that grants access to a resource simply by being presented — the holder (bearer) is assumed to have the right to use it. JWTs are the most common bearer token format. Clients attach them to HTTP requests in the
header. The receiving service cryptographically verifies the token without contacting the issuer on every call.
(JSON Web Signature) — the common case where the payload is signed but readable — and
(JSON Web Encryption) where the payload is both signed and encrypted. In most agent authentication scenarios JWS is used; JWE is reserved for transporting sensitive data inside the token.

Agentic systems involve multiple moving parts: a client (user or orchestrator), an agent API service, background workers, tool runners, external APIs, and often a multi-tenant memory store. Each component needs to know
what they are allowed to do
— without blocking on a shared session database that becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure.
JWTs solve this by embedding identity and permission claims in a cryptographically-verified package that any service can validate independently. A worker receiving a

can instantly confirm the caller's tenant, scope, and expiry from the token — no round-trip to an auth service on the hot path. This is especially important for agentic workloads where hundreds of tool calls may fan out in parallel.
— any service holding the public key can validate a JWT independently; no session store required on the critical path.
— tenant ID, user ID, roles, and scopes travel with the request; workers need not query a user database on every call.
— RFC 7519/7515/7517 are implemented in every major language. Libraries handle parsing, validation, and key rotation automatically.
— the same JWT can be forwarded from the agent API to downstream tool runners, queues, and storage services.
claim provides an automatic, tamper-proof expiry without any server-side state.
) carry authorization policy inside the token, enabling least-privilege enforcement per service.
— a valid JWT cannot be invalidated before its
unless every verifier checks a revocation list or blocklist, which reintroduces server-side state.
— the payload is encoded not encrypted; any token holder can decode it. Never put secrets (API keys, passwords, PII) in a JWS payload.
— a JWT with multiple claims and an RS256 signature can exceed 600–900 bytes; accumulating many claims makes it larger. Small services prefer opaque tokens for header efficiency.
— asymmetric signing requires distributing public keys via JWKS endpoints, rotating private keys, and handling key rollover without downtime.
— expiry validation depends on synchronized clocks. In distributed systems a few seconds of leeway (
parameter) is standard but must be bounded to avoid security gaps.

JWT processing has two sides: issuance (creating and signing the token) and verification (checking it on arrival). Both sides follow a precise algorithmic process defined by the JOSE (JSON Object Signing and Encryption) standards.
Each JWT segment is independently Base64URL-encoded (no padding). The three segments concatenated with dots form the compact serialization:

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9          ← header
.eyJzdWIiOiJ1c2VyLTEyMyIsInRlbmFudCI6ImFjbWUiLCJzY29wZSI6ImFnZW50OnJ1biIsImV4cCI6MTcyMDAwMDAwMCwiaWF0IjoxNzIwMDAwMDAwLCJpc3MiOiJodHRwczovL2F1dGguZXhhbXBsZS5jb20iLCJhdWQiOiJhZ2VudC1hcGkifQ==  ← payload (decoded below)
.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c  ← signature

# Decoded payload:
{
  "sub": "user-123",
  "tenant": "acme",
  "scope": "agent:run",
  "exp": 1720003600,   # unix timestamp
  "iat": 1720000000,
  "iss": "https://auth.example.com",
  "aud": "agent-api"
}
header field selects the signing algorithm. Three families are in common use:
(HMAC-SHA256/512) — symmetric: the same secret signs and verifies. Suitable only when issuer and verifier are the same service; unsafe if verifiers are separate services.
(RSA-SHA256/512) — asymmetric: the private key signs, the public key verifies. Verifiers need only the public key. Standard choice for multi-service deployments.
(ECDSA with P-256/P-521) — asymmetric, shorter signatures than RSA. Preferred for mobile or header-size-sensitive deployments.
— issuer URI; must be validated to prevent token cross-service acceptance.
— intended audience(s); verifier must confirm its own ID is listed.
— expiry (Unix epoch seconds); token invalid after this time.
— issued-at; used to compute token age and to detect replay if combined with a jti check.
— not-before; token not valid before this time (optional, rarely needed).
— unique JWT ID; enables replay prevention via a seen-jti store.
A conforming verifier must perform these checks in order: (1) decode and parse the header; (2) confirm
is in the server's explicit allowlist — never accept
; (3) fetch the signing key (from a local secret or JWKS endpoint); (4) verify the signature over
contains this service's identifier; (8) enforce any application-specific claims (
). Skipping any step is a security vulnerability.

The diagram below shows the complete JWT bearer token flow for a typical agentic deployment: initial authentication against an Auth Server, token issuance via JWKS-backed RS256 signing, and forward propagation of scoped service JWTs to downstream tool runners.

attrs.alt: JWT bearer token flow: client → auth server → agent API → tool runner, with validation steps and JWKS endpoint

content.text: JWTs are well-suited when stateless, scalable authentication is required across service boundaries, when the token lifetime can be kept short, and when all verifiers can be updated to use the same JWKS endpoint.
— clients authenticate once and include the JWT in every subsequent
— the agent API issues a narrowly-scoped service JWT for each downstream tool call, carrying only the claims that tool needs.
in the token lets every service enforce tenant boundaries without database lookups.
— an external IdP (Auth0, Okta, Cognito) issues JWTs; agent services verify them with the IdP's public keys.
— CI/CD pipelines or batch jobs receive a JWT with a 5-minute
to call agent APIs without storing long-lived API keys.

Avoid JWTs when instant revocation is critical (e.g., post-breach token invalidation within seconds), when tokens must carry large payloads (prefer opaque session IDs that reference server-side data), or when all consumers are a single monolithic service that can share an in-process session cache.

A complete JWT implementation spans three responsibilities: issuing signed tokens (auth service), verifying incoming tokens (API services), and forwarding scoped tokens to downstream services (agent worker). The examples below use Python's

# pip install PyJWT cryptography
import jwt
import time
from pathlib import Path

# Load RSA private key (PEM format)
PRIVATE_KEY = Path("/run/secrets/rsa_private.pem").read_text()
ISSUER = "https://auth.example.com"


def issue_token(
    user_id: str,
    tenant_id: str,
    scope: str = "agent:run",
    ttl: int = 3600,
) -> str:
    """Issue a short-lived RS256 JWT for an authenticated user."""
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        "sub": user_id,
        "tenant_id": tenant_id,
        "scope": scope,
        "iss": ISSUER,
        "aud": "agent-api",
        "iat": now,
        "exp": now + ttl,
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, PRIVATE_KEY, algorithm="RS256")
Use a JWKS client that fetches and caches the issuer's public keys. Validate every mandatory claim explicitly.

from jwt import PyJWKClient, PyJWTError
import jwt

JWKS_URL = "https://auth.example.com/.well-known/jwks.json"
# PyJWKClient caches keys and handles rotation automatically
_jwks = PyJWKClient(JWKS_URL, cache_keys=True)


def verify_token(token: str) -> dict:
    """Validate signature, expiry, iss, aud. Raise on any failure."""
    try:
        signing_key = _jwks.get_signing_key_from_jwt(token)
    except Exception as exc:
        raise ValueError(f"JWKS key lookup failed: {exc}") from exc

    return jwt.decode(
        token,
        signing_key.key,
        algorithms=["RS256"],       # explicit allowlist — never ["RS256", "none"]
        audience="agent-api",
        issuer="https://auth.example.com",
        leeway=10,                  # tolerate ±10 s clockskew
    )
Example 1 — Minimal Python round-trip (HS256 for local testing)
This self-contained example shows the full encode/decode cycle using a symmetric secret, suitable for integration tests or local development.

# pip install PyJWT
import jwt, time

SECRET = "dev-only-secret-do-not-use-in-production"

# Issue
payload = {
    "sub": "user-42",
    "scope": "agent:run",
    "iss": "http://localhost:8000",
    "aud": "agent-api",
    "exp": int(time.time()) + 300,
    "iat": int(time.time()),
}
token = jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm="HS256")
print("token:", token[:60], "...")

# Verify
claims = jwt.decode(
    token,
    SECRET,
    algorithms=["HS256"],
    audience="agent-api",
    issuer="http://localhost:8000",
)
print("subject:", claims["sub"])   # → user-42
print("scope:  ", claims["scope"])  # → agent:run
Example 2 — JavaScript fetch with Bearer JWT
Frontend or Node.js clients attach the JWT to every API request. The
package handles both generation and verification; in the browser, token storage goes in memory (not localStorage) to avoid XSS exposure.
A multi-tenant agentic SaaS platform processes tens of thousands of agent runs per hour. Each run is triggered by a user JWT carrying
Users authenticate via an external IdP (Okta) that returns an OIDC ID token. The platform's auth service exchanges this for a shorter-lived platform JWT with tenant-specific claims.
The API gateway validates the JWT using Okta's JWKS endpoint with a 5-minute public key cache. Invalid tokens are rejected at the edge — no request reaches the agent workers.
Each agent worker validates the JWT again (defence in depth) and extracts
to namespace all memory reads and writes.
When invoking a tool runner, the worker issues a new service JWT with scope reduced to the specific tool:
. This prevents tool runners from being used by other paths even if their endpoint is exposed.
The following snippet shows the service JWT issuance for a downstream tool call:

def issue_service_token(
    user_claims: dict,
    tool_name: str,
    ttl: int = 60,  # very short — one tool call
) -> str:
    """Downscoped JWT for a single tool invocation."""
    now = int(time.time())
    return jwt.encode(
        {
            "sub": user_claims["sub"],
            "tenant_id": user_claims["tenant_id"],
            "scope": f"tool:invoke:{tool_name}",
            "parent_jti": user_claims.get("jti"),  # audit trail
            "iss": ISSUER,
            "aud": f"tool-runner.{tool_name}",
            "iat": now,
            "exp": now + ttl,
        },
        PRIVATE_KEY,
        algorithm="RS256",
    )
attack allows an attacker to craft an unsigned token by setting the algorithm to
in the header. Libraries that accept any algorithm from the token header without an explicit allowlist are vulnerable.

# WRONG — allows alg:none attack
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=jwt.algorithms.get_default_algorithms())

# RIGHT — explicit, minimal allowlist
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])

parameters means that a JWT issued by a different service (or for a different audience) is accepted. In multi-service agentic systems this allows token forwarding attacks where a token intended for the tool runner is reused to call the agent API.

# WRONG — no audience or issuer check
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])

# RIGHT — both required
claims = jwt.decode(
    token, key,
    algorithms=["RS256"],
    audience="agent-api",
    issuer="https://auth.example.com",
)
Long-lived tokens without a refresh flow

to 24 hours or longer means a stolen token is valid for a full day. If the token cannot be revoked (no blocklist), an attacker has extended access. Use short-lived access tokens (15–60 minutes) paired with a refresh token flow. Refresh tokens are long-lived but opaque, stored server-side, and revocable.
JavaScript applications that store JWTs in
expose them to XSS attacks. Any injected script can read and exfiltrate the token. Store tokens in memory (a module-level variable or React state) and use HttpOnly cookies for the refresh token.

. The holder (and any intermediary that logs headers) can decode it trivially. Never place API keys, passwords, credit card numbers, or other secrets in a JWS payload. If confidentiality is required, use JWE (JSON Web Encryption, RFC 7516) or store sensitive data server-side and reference it by an opaque ID.
Asymmetric signing means verifiers need only the public key — the private key never leaves the auth service.
with automatic key rotation. Verifiers cache public keys and re-fetch on unknown key IDs (
Set exp to the shortest practical lifetime.
15–60 minutes for access tokens. Use refresh tokens (opaque, stored in HttpOnly cookies) for longer sessions.
Always validate exp, iss, aud, and signature.
Never skip any step, even in internal services.
Issue downscoped service JWTs for each downstream call so that a compromised tool runner cannot call other services.
Add a jti claim and check for replay when needed.
For high-value one-time operations, store seen JTIs in Redis with the token's TTL.
Log the jti, not the full token.
Logging raw JWTs in structured logs exposes bearer credentials. Log only the
header to identify which key was used. During rotation, publish both old and new keys in JWKS until all live tokens expire.

JWT verification is computationally cheap compared to a database session lookup. An RS256 signature verification using a 2048-bit key takes roughly 0.2–0.5 ms in Python (OpenSSL via cryptography library) and under 0.1 ms in Go or Rust. At 10,000 requests per second this adds at most 1–5 ms of CPU time per core — well within budget for a typical agent API.
The main performance consideration is JWKS key fetching. A cold JWKS request to an external IdP takes 20–200 ms. Use a library that caches public keys in memory (PyJWT's

, or jose/jwks-rsa in Node.js) and re-fetches only on cache miss or TTL expiry (typically 5–15 minutes). In Kubernetes, each pod maintains its own key cache; the JWKS endpoint experiences read traffic proportional to the number of pod starts, not request rate.

For extremely high throughput (100k+ RPS), consider verifying tokens at the API gateway layer (nginx + lua-resty-jwt, Envoy + ext_authz, or Kong JWT plugin) so that application pods receive pre-validated requests and skip in-process verification. This shifts the CPU cost to the gateway tier where horizontal scaling is cheap.
— the standard library for JWT encode/decode. Supports all common algorithms.
— broader JOSE standard support including JWE and JWK set management.
— the de-facto JWT library for Node. Synchronous and callback APIs.
— modern zero-dependency JOSE library with JWKS client, JWE, and WebCrypto support. Preferred for browser-side JWT work.
— fast, idiomatic Go JWT library with pluggable key providers.
— managed identity providers that issue OIDC JWTs; expose JWKS endpoints out of the box.
— browser-based JWT debugger for decoding tokens during development. Never paste production tokens.
library in Node.js to verify a JWT from a JWKS endpoint:
Hard — requires blocklist or short exp
Zero-trust service mesh, high-security internal networks
Security-conscious new projects; avoids alg agility

JWT security depends entirely on correct implementation. The cryptographic primitives themselves are sound, but the ecosystem of libraries and the wide configuration surface have produced numerous real-world vulnerabilities. Production systems should treat the following as requirements, not recommendations.
Pin the algorithm allowlist on both issuer and verifier.
The issuer should only ever produce RS256 or ES256. Verifiers should reject any token whose
is not in a hard-coded list.
Rotate private keys on a schedule.
Quarterly or after any credential exposure event. Use
to allow gradual rollover without service interruption.
Implement a token blocklist for critical revocations.
A small Redis set of revoked JTIs (keyed with TTL matching the token lifetime) provides revocation capability when needed (user logout, account compromise).
Encrypt tokens at rest if persisting.
Refresh tokens stored in a database should be hashed (bcrypt or SHA-256) so that a DB breach does not leak usable tokens.
Unlimited token generation enables credential-stuffing attacks. Throttle issuance per user and per IP.
values, or JTIs seen more than once (replay).
Test with expired and malformed tokens.
Include negative test cases that exercise rejection of expired, wrong-audience, wrong-issuer, and tampered tokens.
A JWT is a Base64URL-encoded, dot-separated
bundle — the payload is readable by any holder, but only the issuer's key makes it trustworthy.
Use RS256 or ES256 in production so that verifier services need only the public key, never the signing secret.
in addition to the signature — skipping any step opens the door to cross-service token abuse.
Keep access token lifetimes short (15–60 minutes) and pair them with opaque refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.
Publish a JWKS endpoint and cache public keys in verifiers; this decouples key rotation from service deployments.
Issue downscoped service JWTs for each downstream tool call so that a compromised tool runner cannot escalate privilege.
Never store secrets, passwords, or PII in the JWT payload — it is encoded, not encrypted.
Revocation requires an explicit mechanism (token blocklist or very short exp); plan for it before deployment, not after a breach.
